ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tempeh food product class (FOODON_03302757); no specific fried tempeh platter entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers soya bean products including tempeh; no fried-preparation sub-code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for fried tempeh or tempeh food product at time of processing.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a platter of 6 pieces of fried tempeh, estimated at approximately 120 g total (approximately 20 g per piece), as typically served at Indonesian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Tempe goreng (fried tempeh) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table composite
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for tempeh (NDB 16113) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for fried tempeh. Deep-frying increases fat content relative to raw tempeh; fat values adjusted upward to reflect oil absorption during frying (approximately +8–10 g fat per 100 g versus raw tempeh), consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA fried tempeh reference. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of soybean and soy-based foods — peer-reviewed estimate; tempeh GI reference from University of Sydney GI Database analogous soy food entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for fried tempeh found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 25 based on analogous soy-based fermented food entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which report GI values of 15–25 for soybean and tempeh-type products. Fermentation and high protein/fat content are known to lower GI. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (25 × 16.8) / 100 = 4.2, where carbohydrate per 120 g serving = 14.0 × 1.2 = 16.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~180 mg per 100 g); potassium present at ~412 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Fried tempeh has moderate sodium content; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as kecap manis or sambal, while the potassium content (~412 mg/100 g) may offer a modest counterbalancing benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25) and high protein and dietary fibre content attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried tempeh has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 25 and a glycemic load of approximately 4.2 per standard serving, making it a suitable protein-rich food choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-sugar sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat profile; soy isoflavones and dietary fibre may support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fried tempeh provides predominantly unsaturated fats (monounsaturated and polyunsaturated) with low saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g), and soy isoflavones in tempeh have been associated with modest LDL-cholesterol reduction; however, deep-frying adds total fat and calories, so portion control is advised for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried tempeh (tempe goreng) is a traditional Indonesian dish made from tempeh — a firm cake of whole soybeans bound together by a white mould (Rhizopus oligosporus) through a controlled fermentation process. The tempeh is sliced into rectangular or bite-sized pieces and deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden-brown and crispy on the outside while remaining dense and chewy inside. The flavour is nutty, earthy, and slightly savoury, with a satisfying crunch from the fried exterior. Fried tempeh is one of the most widely consumed protein sources in Indonesian cuisine and is commonly served as a side dish alongside rice, vegetables, and sambal. In Singapore, it appears on the menus of Indonesian and Malay-influenced restaurants and hawker stalls. Nutritionally, fried tempeh is a high-protein, high-fibre food with a favourable unsaturated fat profile. Per 100 g it provides approximately 270 kcal, 18.5 g protein, 16 g fat (predominantly unsaturated), and 14 g carbohydrate with 4.5 g dietary fibre. Its low estimated glycemic index (~25) makes it suitable for blood glucose management. The fermentation process enhances bioavailability of minerals such as iron, zinc, and calcium, and produces vitamin B12 in small amounts. Fried tempeh is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal, making it broadly suitable across dietary requirements. Clinically, it is a low-GI, high-protein food appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease risk, or seeking plant-based protein sources.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried tempeh?
Is fried tempeh good for diabetics?
What does fried tempeh taste like?
How is tempeh different from tofu?
Is fried tempeh high in protein?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (raw tempeh, NDB 16113) and FAO/INFOODS SEA fried tempeh reference data; no exact Singapore restaurant-specific laboratory analysis is available. Fat content has been adjusted upward to account for deep-frying oil absorption. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database soy food entries. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact fried tempeh entry from a primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN) was identified.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent fried tempeh per 100 g, adapted from USDA SR Legacy tempeh entry (NDB 16113) with fat adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption (+8 g fat/100 g) consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA fried tempeh data. 2. Serving size of 120 g (6 pieces at ~20 g each) is an estimate based on typical restaurant portion sizes; actual piece weight may vary. 3. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for fried tempeh was found in the University of Sydney GI Database at time of processing. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy tempeh entry or FAO/INFOODS SEA reference used. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases used. 6. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases used. 7. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases used. 8. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for plant-based frying oil; no industrial trans fats expected. 9. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as tempeh is a plant-based food containing no cholesterol. 10. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; tempeh contains negligible vitamin C and deep-frying destroys any residual trace amounts. 11. The cuisine tag 'Stuffed Pancake / Pancake / Coffee' from the field survey source record reflects the restaurant category (Smile Martabak) rather than the food itself and has been disregarded for classification purposes.
